world_map.raw:
Center point: 52.6338° N, 1.6953° W (Tamworth, Mercia)
Reach radius/trade zone description: Mercia's silver penny system spread across central and southern England and connected via cross-Channel trade to Charlemagne's Frankish realm
Key nodes: Tamworth (52.6338° N, 1.6953° W); Canterbury mint (51.2802° N, 1.0789° E); London mint; Offa's Dyke (Welsh border, ~52.5000° N, 3.1000° W); Aachen (50.7753° N, 6.0839° E)
Suggested map era: 757–796 AD
Historical map source: Cadw Offa's Dyke Resource PDF; Wikimedia Commons Offa's Dyke photography category

quiz.q: Who introduced the 240-penny pound system in England?
What does the £ symbol come from?
quiz.a: The Latin word "Libra," meaning pound weight

quiz.q: Who was the only Anglo-Saxon queen to have coins struck in her own name?
What monetary system did Offa deliberately mirror?
Until what year did Britain's pound-shilling-pence system last?

decision_simulation:
Scenario: It's 793 AD. Offa must decide whether to align Mercia's currency system with the powerful Frankish kingdom's new reform under Charlemagne, risking dependency, or maintain a separate English standard.
Options: (1) Adopt a compatible 240-penny system matching Charlemagne's reform; (2) Keep an independent, incompatible coinage; (3) Negotiate a formal currency treaty
Historical outcome: Offa adopted a closely mirrored system, enabling smoother cross-Channel trade — and that basic structure survived for nearly 1,200 years, until 1971
EXPLORE IT (CURRENTLY OPEN — highest priority)

understand.economic_impact:
One-line summary: Charlotte represents the moment England got its first truly standardized money system — one so well-designed it lasted, almost unchanged, for nearly 1,200 years.
Three key economic shifts: (1) Offa's 240-penny pound created a single trusted unit of value across Mercia and beyond; (2) matching the Frankish system made cross-Channel trade far easier, boosting commerce; (3) royal control over minting (with harsh penalties for counterfeiting) made English money more trustworthy than ad-hoc local coinages
Modern parallel: modern national currencies with central bank oversight and legal tender laws — the same principle of one trusted, government-backed unit of value
understand.political_impact:
One-line summary: Controlling the currency was one of the clearest ways a king could show — and prove — his power over the whole kingdom.
Three key political shifts: (1) Minting coins with the king's name and image became a direct tool of royal authority and propaganda; (2) Cynethryth's coinage broke convention by naming a queen, a small but real shift in how power could be visually represented; (3) matching currency systems with Charlemagne's Francia signaled Mercia's status as a serious European power
Modern parallel: national currencies today still carry political symbolism — whose face is on the money is still a statement about power and identity
understand.why_it_still_matters:
Plain-language answer: The idea of "100 pennies make a pound" (or 100 cents make a dollar) traces back to decisions made over a thousand years ago by a king trying to make trade easier and taxes fairer. Money systems that feel permanent today were actually just really good ideas that stuck around for centuries.
Connection to today's world: Every time you split a bill into smaller units — dollars and cents, pounds and pence — you're using the same basic logic Offa set up in the 8th century, just updated for modern math.
